issue_title: Incorrect Documentation of ID parameter for generate_{service}_shared_access_signature
issue_body:
For the BlobService, FileService and QueueService, the docstring for the ID parameter incorrectly states that to created a stored access policy, use the `set_{service}_service_properties` method. However, this method is used for settings like the CORS and logging, not stored access policies. The TableService correctly states that you should use `set_table_acl`. 

Additionally, they say you can use an ID to one of these access policies, but I have not found a way to actually get the ID of a stored access policy. It is not part of the ACL object and doesn't appear as a collection in object to which the policy is associated. What is the format for this ID?

issue_title: Cannot install due to futures issue
issue_body:
This is a duplicate of: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/2559

The root issue is with this dependency. Nothing seems to have changed lately, either in this, or in the futures package.

Current dependency on azure-storage-common is defined as:
```python
'azure-storage-common>=1.1.0,<1.2.0'
```
This is not the semver recommendation. According to semver, minor version cannot be broken version, and then pin at the bug fix version is incorrect.
This makes your package impossible to install with azure-storage 1.3.0 released yesterday, even if versioning says it's possible.
This breaks at the same time the "azure" bundle, which assumes all internal dependencies follow semver:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/2818

Totally not trying to be snarky here-- the project is called _pre-commit_, after all-- but is there a reason commit-msg hooks are not supported? 

Being able to validate commit messages for appropriate issue-tracking ids, length, etc is important for a lot of us and having to put together another mechanism to deal with it or utilize another framework alongside pre-commit seems non-DRY-y.

I would put this as a "pro" for overcommit (see #384) but I'd really like to see it in pre-commit, as overcommit's performance in Windows is... subpar. (More of a ruby issue than overcommit.)

I found out a fix: adding `shell=True` to `popen_kwargs` in `util.py:cmd_output`.

Now I can submit a pull request, but the question is :
- should we let the user configure "manually" a `system` hook with `shell=True` in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` (this is my preference, but this is a quite obscure setting option we should document clearly)
- should `util.py:cmd_output` detect automagically that we are using a shell and set this flag itself (a bit dodgy maybe)
- should we add a new type of "shell" hook, very similar to "system" with only this little difference (IMHO: probably overkill and a lotta more code to add)

What do you think ?

issue_title: Pybullet - request Features: rescale mesh once loaded
issue_body:
Hi,

Is there a way to rescale a (visual) mesh once it has been loaded? The documentation in the "Pybullet Quickstart Guide" mentions `changeVisualShape` however there are no parameters to change the scale. Currently,  when I want to change the dimensions of a visual shape (for instance a sphere), I removed it from pybullet and then reload it with a different scale. This is not really practical, and it would probably be better to expose this feature from the `changeVisualShape` method.

Thanks.

Could you add the User Manual pdf to the git repository? Maybe in docs? next to the already very useful one from Siggraph.
It always takes me a while to find the pdf in it's latest version (which I believe is this one:
http://code.google.com/p/bullet/source/browse/trunk/Bullet_User_Manual.pdf ).

This page could also benefit from a direct link to the pdf:
http://bulletphysics.org/mediawiki-1.5.8/index.php/Bullet_User_Manual_and_API_documentation

Thanks

Trying to build `html` documentation on Win64 I've faced couple of errors on missing dependencies. They are: `sphinx-gallery`, `sphinx_rtd_theme`. With these packages installed the documentation is being built flawlessly.

with

issue_title: CIMultiDict copy not working as expected
issue_body:
There is an issue with ``CIMultiDict`` copy method
```python
import multidict
multidict.__version__  # '3.1.1'
a = multidict.CIMultiDict()
a['foo'] = 6
b = a.copy()
b['foo'] = 7
b # <CIMultiDict('foo': 7)>
a # <CIMultiDict('foo': 7)>
```

1) Expose REST api for command executions using aiohttp
2) Expose web page  with form and execute button

Method `RoutesDebugPanel.populate` tries to set `DebugPanel.data` property, which is marked as `aiohttp.helpers.reify`. Since aiohttp 0.17.0 (KeepSafe/aiohttp@51ea69d4c863b9a184293371b24e8d3e6651eb25), reified properties are readonly, so this assignment throws `AttributeError`.

This is a neat tool, but whats the equivalent of patch(1) for this xmldiff?

I was hoping to use this patch type output to update XML configs during an ansible playbook...

Hello,

I have been trying for the past week to use Pocketsphinx in iOS.  I do not want to use OpenEars because OpenEars does not allow for bluetooth audio, even though the have disable bluetooth flags that do not appear to work; and I do not have the added flexibility to using an AudioSession as I would like with OpenEars.

I have cloned both Sphinxbase and Pocketsphinx, built both from source through the autogen.sh, and the ./configure - make - make install method (I mean I have tried them both.) . 
I also run the ./bulid-iPhone.sh in both the Sphinxbase and Pocketsphinx directories successfully.

I have edited the Header Search Path, Library Seach Path, and Other Linker Flags in Xcode. 

I am able to build (CMD+B) successfully, however when I run my application, I am getting the following message:

dyld: Library not loaded: /Users/sean/Documents/cmusphinx/pocketsphinx/bin/arm64/lib/libpocketsphinx.3.dylib

I manually added this .dylib to my project, and it still generates the error.

What exactly am I missing here?  Please get back to me as soon as you can.  I really need to utilized the software in this manner because of certain issues we have had with OpenEars and we need to move from it. Please get back to me as soon as you can.

Thanks.

The current background color formatting can clash with different terminal emulator color profiles. For example, see the output in this image, which is a screenshot from my terminal (Fedora 27, default emulator, monokai dark color profile).
https://i.imgur.com/EuJTEXL.png
If possible, don't specify a background color, or use general codes which override a user's color profile for a consistent look an all platforms.

issue_title: Students in multiple courses have to keep editing their nbgrader_config.py
issue_body:
I have a lot of students this year in multiple courses attempting to use nbgrader on jupyterhub (between Physics, Biology, and Computer Science). It appears that every time a student wants to see the Assignments tab for a particular course, they have to edit their nbgrader_config.py file:

```python
c = get_config()
c.NbGrader.course_id = "cs245"
c.TransferApp.exchange_directory = "/opt/nbgrader/exchange/"
```

This is really a pain, and I'd rather not have students even know about such things, let alone have to edit the file. 

Suggestion: make the *system* know what students are in a course, and show the students all courses that they are enrolled in. 

Complication: because there is only one course now (course_id) how will the system know what ID to use for fetch/submission. The UI should know.

issue_title: Jupyter does not respect the default nbconvert template
issue_body:
By adding the line
```
c.LatexExporter.template_file = 'my_template.tplx'
```
to the jupyter_nbconvert_config.py file, I've set nbconvert to default to my template for latex and pdf conversions. However, when I use the "export as pdf" option from within Jupyter notebook it still tries to use the default "article.tplx" template.

HI, 


this is an idea for enhancement:

why not using the fact that you can press more times <kbd> tab </kbd> , after the autocompletion window appears? 

For instance, in Rstudio, by pressing a 2nd time you insert the 1st suggestion of the list (this is very handy). 
Another possibility would be to use <kbd> tab </kbd> to cycle through the suggestions.

It seems to me that the $PATH env variable is messed up when the CentOS image is used.
```
/bin:/sbin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin
```
This happens only when a shell from Jupyter is opened. `$PATH` looks good in a shell created by `docker exec -it docker_id /bin/bash`.

issue_title: When using PageUp/PageDown in Completer, codemirror mistakenly uses goPageUp/goPageDown.
issue_body:
When viewing code hints (dropdown list of available methods/attributes) for an object, if I navigate the list using page down (instead of arrow keys), it navigates the list as desired, but has the undesired side effect of  also moving the text cursor down the page (as if you'd pressed page down while not using code hints). Resultingly, after you press enter to confirm your attribute, the cursor is one page below the line you were just working on. 

Here is the commit that added the page-up and page-down functionality to the completer dropdown. [https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/commit/fe37c14bdf2c0507a2aaf861c6d2be855632b85b](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/commit/fe37c14bdf2c0507a2aaf861c6d2be855632b85b)

> IPython 5.1.0 -- An enhanced Interactive Python.
> Jupyter Notebook Server 4.2.3.

issue_title: Clicking the widget holding the kernel name could do something useful
issue_body: I often witness beginners click on the widget displaying the kernel name, expecting something kernel-related to happen. A natural action would be to open a drop-down for changing the kernel. Or maybe the full kernel menu.

issue_title: Multiple carriage returns are not handled correctly in v4.3
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:
```
>>> print('1\r2')  # yas!
2
>>> print('1\r2\r3')  # wat?
23
```

Potential culprit: pull request #1836

issue_title: Security docs still describe old signature system
issue_body:
We haven't updated the description of the signature system, in `docs/source/security.rst`, since we stored signatures in notebook metadata. They're now stored in an SQLite database. The section on 'collaboration' therefore gives instructions that will no longer work.

Prompted by [this SO question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40136923/how-to-enable-shared-trust-in-jupyter-notebook).

issue_title: Help>Keyboard Shortcuts: Icons appear as upper case letters
issue_body:
I'm learning Notebook, so I thought I'd note something that might trip up other noobs...

issue_title: Segfault when changing type in external variables
issue_body:
If you compile a rule set in yara-python with defined external variables, then send it different types when matching (ie. you give it an int instead of a string) yara can segfault. If you give it an int instead of a string, it will pass that int to strlen as a pointer to the string to read:

```
#!/usr/bin/python                                          
                                                           
import yara                                                
                                                           
external_vars = {                                          
    "test1": "",                                           
    "test2": 0                                             
}                                                          
                                                           
buggy_exts = {                                             
    "test1": 0xDEADBEEF,                                   
    "test2": 1                                             
}                                                          
                                                           
rules = yara.compile("test.rules", externals=external_vars)
m = rules.match("test.rules", externals=buggy_exts)
```

Will result in something like this(from gdb):

```
(gdb) run test.py
Starting program: /usr/bin/python test.py
[Thread debugging using libthread_db enabled]
Using host libthread_db library "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libthread_db.so.1".

Program received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
strlen () at ../sysdeps/x86_64/strlen.S:106
106	../sysdeps/x86_64/strlen.S: No such file or directory.
(gdb) i r
rax            0xdeadbeef	3735928559
rbx            0xdeadbeef	3735928559
...
```

In most (all?) cases you should be able to sanitize this input before it gets sent to yara, but that should not (IMO) be necessary - an error message or warning would be fine, but segfaulting is a bit bad.

Edit: This is with latest commit in git. 3.4 release also has this behaviour.
Edit 2: And the contents of `test.rules` doesn't matter.

issue_title: Trouble with higher order gradients wrt list arguments

issue_body:
In our setup, connections to the RabbitMQ server seem to be less than 100% reliable. I am not sure why this is, but the retry options seem to have addressed most problems. However, it turns out to be the case that when publishing a message, unless there is a connection problem, AMQP itself does not guarantee that your message has been received *by the AMQP server* after the client's publish call completes. This explains why sometimes, Pulsar's status updates seem to be going in to a void.

Transactions are an AMQP standard and would potentially solve this, but not terribly popular and there was discussion of removing them from RabbitMQ entirely. The RabbitMQ preferred solution for guaranteeing that a message has been received is using [Publisher Confirms](http://www.rabbitmq.com/blog/2011/02/10/introducing-publisher-confirms/). However, in testing this feature, I seem to be able to enable it, but I can't find a way to actually handle the "not acknowledged" messages that I think are being generated (unless they are not - debugging this is infuriatingly complicated)...

Using Pulsar's `PulsarExchange` class, one can publish a message like so:

```
exchange.publish('queue_amqp_test', {'msg':'foo'}, connection_kwargs={'transport_options':{'confirm_publish':True}})
```

Do that in a loop and the rate of delivery will be much slower than without `confirm_publish` set, which I believe suggests that the confirms are being sent. However, when the [queue is killed to generate the nacks](http://rabbitmq.1065348.n5.nabble.com/Testing-lightweight-publisher-confirms-td834.html), the messages just keep going into the void, no exceptions are raised, and, if an `on_return` callback is set on the `Producer` object [as the fine manual suggests might kinda be relevant except maybe not since it's maybe for the *mandatory* or *immediate* arguments which it then later says are not supported anyway](http://kombu.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/kombu.html#message-producer), that callback is never fired.

Because of this, and the fact that confirms are a RabbitMQ-specific feature anyway, I think we may need to solve this problem application-side by having Galaxy send messages back to Pulsar acknowledging receipt, like so:

```
+-----------------+
| Pulsar          |
+-----------------+
 |   |   ^   |   ^       
[1] [2]  |  [2]  |       
 |   |  [3]  |  [4] 
 v   v   |   v   |
+-----------------+
| Galaxy          |
+-----------------+

[1]: status update sent but not received
[2]: status update resent after some wait, and received
[3]: acknowledgement of status update sent but not received
[4]: acknowledgement of status update sent and received
```

Only after `[4]` can Pulsar give up on attempting to send the message.

In addition to the changes to Pulsar, Galaxy will need to:

1. Handle the case of duplicate or out-of-order status update messages
2. Perform the same series of operations for sending new job and kill messages

**Description of problem**: login fault when try to auth with Active Directory and _user search base configured to domain/forest root_. 

**Error text in log**:

> INFO - POST-3- LDAP search returned too many (4) results for 'username'

**Error reason**: dirty _conn.response_ lenght check. There not only 'searchResEntry' type entries may present in LDAP responce. There may be 'searchResRef' type entries (most often occur in MS AD, **but can also be found in other LDAP servers**). In Active Directory forest root there always 3 refferal links, so in LDAP response, 4 objects are obtained: one user object with type 'searchResEntry' and tree 'searchResRef' objects.

**Solution**: count in _conn.response_  only 'searchResEntry' type entries, and (in the ideal case) correctly handle LDAP referral links.

issue_title: Inputs search does not return any scores
Stripping curly brackets is too greedy
issue_body:
In #158 a customization to strip `{` and `}` from fields was introduced. The problem with the greedy approach in the current implementation is that it will also replace curly brackets in mathematical expressions, and these are occasionally used in titles of papers.

The solution would be to only replace curly brackets in text mode, i.e. iterate over the string and keep track of text or math mode, and only then replace curly brackets.

If you ignore the `abstract` field, then the only thing you need to worry about is `$` or `\(` and `\)` for inline math. Inside the `abstract` field (and maybe similar fields if there are any), anything can happen, and one is royally screwed in coming up with an implementation.

issue_title: No decorator to run handler for every hook
issue_body:
There is no mechanism provided to declare that a handler should be run during the hook phase for each and every hook.

The most obvious fix would be adjusting the existing `@hook` decorator so that it always runs if no patterns are provided (and an argument never passed to the handler).

In my experience, charms should  minimize code that can only run in a specific hook to avoid race conditions. For example, consider a simple service configuration change. This will queue the config-changed hook on each unit, but if there is already a queue of hooks being processed by a unit then these hooks will see the changed configuration in the hook environment before the config-changed hook has had a chance to run. This can be fatal, with hooks crashing attempting to run code paths before the config-changed hook has set things up so they can be run successfully. Similar races can be described for leadership and relations. The simplest way of avoiding this entire class of races is to have a single, general hook instead of several specific ones tied to particular events.

Before, I used to be able to use shorthand such as:

```
sshuttle -r ... 192.168.208/20
```

Or:

```
sshuttle -r ... 10/8
```

And these would be expanded to `192.168.208.0/20` or `10.0.0.0/8` respectively.

Since https://github.com/sshuttle/sshuttle/commit/c4a41ada09ec6fbfeb1783eb0269cad013842982 it appears that this behavior has changed when piping the address through `socket.getaddrinfo`:

```
>>> socket.getaddrinfo('10', 0, 0, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
[(2, 1, 6, '', ('0.0.0.10', 0))]
```

`socket.getaddrinfo` seems to not zero fill on the right hand side like the old code used to do.

I'm not sure if this is intended/correct behavior, or if it just worked magically before by accident, but it seems like it'd be relatively easy to restore this behavior by padding the string with `.0`'s before passing into `socket.getaddrinfo`. I'll submit a PR to go along with this.
What Python versions does it support?

issue_body:
I'm a contributor of  `Microsoft/vsts-tasks` repository. We have a `Dotnet Core Installer` task to aid the users to install a specific version of dotnet Runtime/SDK. For helping the user to figure out the versions, we added https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/release-notes/releases.csv to our help text. 

We are facing some issues, because of some mismatches in the .csv file. Here's a GitHub issue link: https://github.com/Microsoft/vsts-tasks/issues/7032 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25762922/41221081-ce5dd986-6d80-11e8-92d0-ff76394ed798.png)

Although, the version-sdk here says `2.1.0-preview2`, it should have been `2.1.300-preview2-008533` instead (based on the urls) or something along those lines. 

Addressing this issue, we assumed we could rely on the [Releases.json](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/release-notes/releases.json) for mapping a display-value to the download URL, but given the inconsistency between the CSV file and JSON file, it would result in failures when using `2.x` (~preview) versions.

issue_title: Default DTSTART in rrule lacks a timezone
issue_body:
Since 2.7, the following code fails in our project:

```python
rule = rrule.rrulestr("RRULE:FREQ=WEEKLY;WKST=MO;BYDAY=MO,TU;UNTIL=20180331T200000Z")
```

This is the error we see:

```python-traceback
ValueError: RRULE UNTIL values must be specified in UTC when DTSTART is timezone-aware
```

The comment around the exception mentions this:

```python
# According to RFC5545 Section 3.3.10:
# https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5545#section-3.3.10
#
# > If the "DTSTART" property is specified as a date with UTC
# > time or a date with local time and time zone reference,
# > then the UNTIL rule part MUST be specified as a date with
# > UTC time.
```

We however do not specify the DTSTART property. Dateutil defaults to the current date in this case. However, said default does not have a timezone. Because we do however pass a timezone with our UNTIL property, we run into this exception.

Now I think you can make the argument that this is really our problem and it's probably best anyway to be specific with the DTSTART property (which is how we're going to work around this problem). However, I think it might also make sense to apply the timezone of the UNTIL property when the DTSTART date is created by dateutil.

This is related to #620 and #634.

issue_title: [improvement] Add feature to list all serial ports available on the system (multi-platform)
issue_body:
I saw a good answer @tfeldmann for a generic issue of pyserial [here](http://stackoverflow.com/a/14224477/3753497)

```python
import sys
import glob

import serial


def serial_ports():
    """ Lists serial port names

        :raises EnvironmentError:
            On unsupported or unknown platforms
        :returns:
            A list of the serial ports available on the system
    """
    if sys.platform.startswith('win'):
        ports = ['COM%s' % (i + 1) for i in range(256)]
    elif sys.platform.startswith('linux') or sys.platform.startswith('cygwin'):
        # this excludes your current terminal "/dev/tty"
        ports = glob.glob('/dev/tty[A-Za-z]*')
    elif sys.platform.startswith('darwin'):
        ports = glob.glob('/dev/tty.*')
    else:
        raise EnvironmentError('Unsupported platform')

    result = []
    for port in ports:
        try:
            s = serial.Serial(port)
            s.close()
            result.append(port)
        except (OSError, serial.SerialException):
            pass
    return result


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(serial_ports())
```

My question for pyserial board is:
Will accept a PR with these feature?
Or Do you prefer a separated pypi package for just this feature?

- When I force quit, restart mpsyt and search for a playlist (if the problem was with a song query) or a song (if the issue was with a playlist query) I do get back results for this latest query. 

- Updating mpsyt does not work.
- Sometimes a reboot helps.

issue_title: It is still possible to subvert __setattr__ with some GangaObject inherited classes
issue_body:
This has been a problem which appears to come and go and I've plugged a few areas in the GangaObject and Proxy code where the situation arises.

The problem is basically that a member attribute of a class (which happens to be in the Schema but strictly this shouldn't matter!) fails to have it's _parent attribute assigned upon the attribute being assigned.

I've added some _setParent and #FIXME statements into the Job class (https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/commit/9be4d2b58e5a6b0a616e166fd720c29e13084b64) which should be removed and un-needed by the next release.

This 'fix', whilst works, concerns me that this may be broken in more places in the testing or in some usage I'm not currently testing (i.e. maybe the atlas env or similar).


I've debugged this particular problem multiple times before and it often turns out to be some init/construct/(some-other) method which oversteps itself and assigns an item directly subverting the setter methods in the GangaObject.

My plan is to try and trace the setter/getter methods to check for either an assignment which fails to correctly assign a parent to an attribute of GangaObject type and/or to make sure that without a proxy that the bare class always calls the getter/setter methods as intended without the possibility of these methods being ignored for the standard python methods.

In latest develop
```python
from Ganga.GPIDev.Base.Proxy import addProxy, stripProxy
l = LocalFile()
assert l is addProxy(stripProxy(l))
```
does not pass but it used to, even as recent as 6.1.14. This was broken I think in #163 (specifically 55d7098cdac079) where I pointed it out but didn't realise the extent of the change. It was presented then as a performance change but really we want to be careful about object identity breaking.

I think that for correctness and compatibility with previous version of Ganga I would like to change this behaviour back by attaching a `_proxyObject` attribute to a `GangaObject` from Proxy.py if it's been wrapped. Of course we want to avoid `GangaObject` having any knowledge of this and so I also propose changing the "do not copy" logic in Objects.py to an "only copy these" logic.

This is not important enough to warrant a change in 6.1.16 but would make sense soon after.

issue_title: idwtn doesn't tolerate unequal shapes in coefficients
issue_body:
The following two snippets of code should be equivalent, I think:
```
import numpy as np
import pywt

a = np.random.randn(64,64)
w = pywt.Wavelet('bior3.3')
#w = pywt.Wavelet('db2')
#w = pywt.Wavelet('db1')

coeffs  = pywt.dwtn(a,w)
coeffs2 = pywt.dwtn(coeffs['aa'], w)
coeffs['aa'] = pywt.idwtn(coeffs2, w)
print coeffs['aa'].shape, coeffs['dd'].shape
#b1 = pywt.idwtn(coeffs, w)


coeffs = pywt.dwt2(a,w)
coeffs2 = pywt.dwt2(coeffs[0], w)
coeffs = pywt.idwt2(coeffs2, w), coeffs[1]
print coeffs[0].shape, coeffs[1][0].shape
b2 = pywt.idwt2(coeffs, w)

#print np.max(np.fabs(b1-b2))
```

However, if one uses 'db2' or 'bior3.3' wavelet (the only one that I know works is 'db1'), the first version crashes in the commented line (that generates b1), because not all coefficients have the same shape.

issue_title: Streaming at high framerate: fps not accurate?
issue_body:
Camera model: SR300
OS: Linux (Ubuntu 14.04, Kernel 4.4)

I have a question about frame access at high speeds (possibly silly; I'm not fluent in C++ and may be missing something obvious)

- I'm trying to store high-resolution colour images at 30fps, and depth images at a rather lower framerate
- These do not have to be synchronised.
- I'm not sure if librealsense allows storage of the native RSSDK format available in the Windows SDK, however we require at least an hour's footage at high framerate, and given the large filesizes associated with this format, it's in any case not feasible for us. 
- So I've written a rather naive program which creates and detaches a new thread to store each incoming frame either as an image or a binary file (depending on stream type), and this in principle works quite well.

However, that said, I'm running into issues at high framerates. Specifically, the command
dev->wait_for_frames 
limits the framerate to about 8 or 9fps (verified by pointing the sensor at an external clock). This is true **even when only a colour stream is enabled,** so I do not think it is a synchronisation issue. It is also not a computational speed issue, because the threaded processing reduces the processing loop time to well under the requisite 33ms (and indeed i have confirmed this, by running the loop on a 'frozen' image frame). So it does not seem to be an problem with the image storage - it's the streaming itself that is not running fast enough. Querying the framerate returns 30fps, as requested - but clearly this is not true in fact. 

Without using wait_for_frames, I can store images at an appropriate framerate as measured by clock time, but the stored frames are either incomplete or totally blank, so I don't think this is a valid solution. We were relying on the high framerate offered by the RealSense to gather high-resolution tracking data in RGB-D, and have only a few weeks per year where this is possible (we are now in the field and the clock has started ticking!) So help/suggestions urgently needed and much appreciated!

issue_body:
Attempting to use axes with combination user and ip has resulted in the entire site being unreachable, every single page simply showing the plain message: `get() returned more than one User -- it returned 2!`

This is before a user has actually logged into django at all. Our system allows for multiple `None` usernames as not every user actually needs to be able to log in, while it appears axes has (admittedly fairly) assumed that there will never be more than one user with a blank username.

I haven't dug any further into this, if there is some configuration that will allow axes to work with our system then that would be wonderful.

    mode='constant', constant_values=0)

    pad_width = _validate_lengths(narray, pad_width)
  File "C:\Users\J\Anaconda3\envs\tensorflow-gpu\lib\site-packages\numpy\lib\arraypad.py", line 1080, in _validate_lengths
    normshp = _normalize_shape(narray, number_elements)

    raise ValueError(fmt % (shape,))
ValueError: Unable to create correctly shaped tuple from ((14, 14), (14, 14), (0, 0))
```

It appears to assume your numpy array is 3 dimension (ie an RGB) image.

To get it to run, I had to modify data_augmentation.py line so that:
`        npad = ((padding, padding), (padding, padding), (0, 0))
`read
`        npad = ((padding, padding), (padding, padding))
`
Then it works perfectly.

I'm trying to train deep net on a big dataset that doesn't fit into memory. 
Is there any way to use generators to read batches into memory on every training step?

batch_gen = generator(data)

issue_title: Improve how command output gets piped to shell command

issue_body:
## Issue description

On recent enough Python versions (I think 3.4+), non-overloaded Python functions created by pybind11 could set the `__text_signature__` attribute to a textual representation of the function signature, instead of prepending it to the docstring (see `inspect._signature_from_builtin` in the stdlib). This attribute is recognized (and parsed) by at least pydoc and inspect.signature (and thus, I guess, Sphinx too, though I am not sure), so it would perform the task of communicating the function signature better than the current method.

(Originally posted on gitter, it was suggested that I put the proposal here.)

issue_title: Feature suggest: Use 2048-bit or stronger Diffie-Hellman groups
issue_body:
Hello,

Since now the ciphers for [apache](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/0.15.x/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/options-ssl-apache.conf) and [nginx](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/0.15.x/certbot-nginx/certbot_nginx/options-ssl-nginx.conf) contains `DHE-*`, it is recommended to use 2048-bit or stronger Diffie-Hellman groups.

For example on Nginx and CentOS:

```
ssl_ciphers "ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:ECDHE-RSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-SHA:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-SHA:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:DHE-RSA-AES256-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES256-SHA:ECDHE-ECDSA-DES-CBC3-SHA:ECDHE-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA:EDH-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA:AES128-GCM-SHA256:AES256-GCM-SHA384:AES128-SHA256:AES256-SHA256:AES128-SHA:AES256-SHA:DES-CBC3-SHA:!DSS";
ssl_dhparam /etc/ssl/dhparam.pem;
```

But the path to `dhparams.pem` may vary. Does anybody familiar with this?

---

References:

- https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/analyze.html
- https://mozilla.github.io/server-side-tls/ssl-config-generator/
- https://weakdh.org/sysadmin.html

The one-command approach is really great! It would be awesome to see this implemented for a wide range of tasks as well. Possible scenarios would be:

{dwimbot,letsdwim} --coffee
{dwimbot,letsdwim} --fix
{dwimbot,letsdwim} --money
{dwimbot,letsdwim} --cleanup

I'd be happy to discuss ideas!

## My operating system is (include version):

ArchLinux, recently updated.

```
Linux radio.kousu.ca 4.10.2-1-ARCH #1 SMP PREEMPT Mon Mar 13 17:13:41 CET 2017 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

`pacman -S certbot-nginx certbot`, current versions:

```
community/certbot 0.12.0-1 [installed]

issue_title: gpg sig check for certain requests
issue_body:
For things like [bug 1394486](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1394486), where we'd have to be able to submit signing requests for arbitrary binaries, it would be nice to protect it with a gpg-signed request.

So:
- submit request with a gpg-signed token
- tiny graph of decision task -> focus signing task
- verify cot as usual
- verify the original request token has a valid gpg signature
- then proceed with signing the apk

## Problem Definition

We should have a standardized way to minimally "self test" a raiden build/installation. That would allow to
- minimize debugging search space/help pointing out installation environment problems
- safe guard against regressions from refactoring (CLI is not extensively tested in CI)
- fail faster in CI
- have a reasonable testing path for binary bundles (the `AppImage`) in CI

## Solution

The idea is to have a `raiden smoketest` command in CLI that will test the general sanity of the installation. In detail it would

    - start `geth` in a `tempdir` on a random free port
    - use a known `account`
    - use a `smoketest_genesis` that has the raiden contracts deployed and some channels
        already opened (genesis should be created on `python setup.py install` )
    - connect to the contracts
    - test `EndpointRegistry` (register our own endpoint)
    - test channel collection from Registry
    - test Transport available
        - try to setup transport (open a port)
        - try to setup protocol on top of transport
        - try to send a ping to our own port (?) (could be a channel partner with the same
            endpoint pre-registered in `smoketest_genesis` )
    - shut down

The smoketest can later be expanded to include other features like WAL, snapshotting, recovery.

## Problem Definition

The NettingChannelLibrary has the `settled` attribute for the settled block. But at the block where we settle, the contract also performs a self-destruct, so this attribute is utterly useless.

## Solution

Remove it and all references to it.

## Problem Definition
Latest geth version (1.6.1) breaks some of our tests.

## Solution

Investigate why this happens and fix it.


## Tasklist


- [ ] Find out and fix the reason why tests fail with geth 1.6.1
- [ ] Bump travis to use geth 1.6.1

issue_title: Moving actions and targets from language modules to sos.
issue_body:
Right now, if we would like to execute sos workflow on a remote host, we will need to install `sos`, `sos-notebook`, and language modules for actions such as `python`. `sos-notebook` is needed because local sos could execute `sos preview -q` to get preview of remote files and the previewers are implemented in `sos-notebook`. Two recent releases have addressed this problem so that `sos-notebook` is no longer needed for remote preview.

However, `sos-notebook` is still needed because it is required by language modules, which provide not only the actions and targets (e.g. `bash`, `R_Library`), but also languages modules (e.g. support for `bash_kernel`). In theory, remote execution will need the actions and targets, but not language modules, but they are currently bundled together.

So the situation now is that we will have to install all sos modules on remote servers. It is not a big deal but incorrect installation (e.g. missing `sos-bash`) would disallow the execution of tasks on the remote host. My proposal is to move all actions and targets to the SoS module (sos workflow engine) so that installing `sos` will install all workflow related functions.

This solution is not clean in the sense that we will have to modify `sos` for the addition of actions for new languages, but I do not know a better way.

#488 has been implemented but it is up to the user to add the restrictions to their `.hosts.yml` file. It is possible to allow system admin to configure the server and enforce certain restrictions. For example, in `/etc/sos.yml`,

```
server_ip:
    process:
        max_mem: 4G
    PBS:
        max_mem: 64G
```

sos could check such file before the task is started.

Note that system admin can enforce the task restrictions manually, by stating `max_mem` etc as policy, and check the tasks with commands such as `sos status task_id -v4 | grep max_mem`.

issue_title: Confusing help message when incorrectly using logging flags
issue_body:
When you put a logging flag between `pulumi` and the verb you're intending to log, you get a funny help message that doesn't line up with reality:

```
$ pulumi --logflow update
flag provided but not defined: -logflow
Usage of pulumi:
  -C string
    	Run pulumi as if it had been started in another directory
  -alsologtostderr
    	log to standard error as well as files
  -log_backtrace_at value
    	when logging hits line file:N, emit a stack trace
  -log_dir string
    	If non-empty, write log files in this directory
  -logtostderr
    	log to standard error instead of files
  -stderrthreshold value
    	logs at or above this threshold go to stderr
  -v value
    	log level for V logs
  -vmodule value
    	comma-separated list of pattern=N settings for file-filtered logging
```

`pulumi update --logflow` works since Cobra parses it correctly.

issue_title: N^2 problem when registering custom classes.
issue_body:
Whenever a custom class is serialized by Ray for the first time on a worker (or driver), the class definition is pickled and shipped to all of the other workers (and the driver).

So if the same class definition is serialized on all of the workers, it will be exported to all of the other workers every single time, resulting in `N^2` messages, where `N` is the number of workers. This can be inspected as follows (on a single machine).

```python
import ray
import time

N = 10

ray.init(num_cpus=N)

class Foo(object):
    pass

@ray.remote
def f():
    time.sleep(0.1)
    return Foo()

time.sleep(3)

for _ in range(N):
    f.remote()

time.sleep(5)

# Then see how many things have been "exported".
exports = ray.worker.global_worker.redis_client.lrange('Exports', 0, -1)

print(len(exports))  # This should be something like N + 6 (assuming 
                     # that all N tasks got scheduled on different workers).
```

When the number of workers is in the thousands (e.g., 5000 across a cluster), this seems to congest the primary Redis shard a lot (leading to other kinds of failures).

issue_title: Support for bootstrap4-alpha5 left column labels for checkboxes.
issue_body:
Bootstrap4 now supports labels for checkbox (#658), which I find very useful thing. For example you can have label "Agreement" and near checkbox write add another label which is more explaining. In such way form design/layout isn't getting broken with gaps.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/445304/20827879/3836f0a0-b87d-11e6-83a3-5965d4738d6a.png)

In terms of consistency I think current label should go into left label column, then all fields would have consistency and no exceptions and label near checkbox input should be optional. By doing this we could also get rid of bunch of exceptions in field templates and managing offset for central column as well.

The questions are:
1. Do you agree, that main label go into left column?
2. If yes, then which variable we should use for label near checkbox?
3. Maybe other suggestions?

First idea that came to my mind is - we could use `help_text` as label near checkbox field. Although this would make an exception and help text for checkboxes would have different look. 
On the other hand do someone really need label near input AND help text right below?

issue_title: No way to specify chunking for string variables when writing to netcdf
issue_body:
`datacube.storage.netdcf_writer.create_variable`

silently truncates chunk size that have too many dimensions, then for string variables adds extra dimension back in by converting them from 1d array of strings to 2d array of bytes

https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/be5d9acad74429aebab3a48856c324b103fc3b4e/datacube/storage/netcdf_writer.py#L99-L102

Code above intends to clamp each element of `chunksizes` to maximum size in that dimension, but because of the use of `zip` it also truncates to number of dimensions present in the original variable (which lacks extra `_nchar` dimension) 

Later on exception will be thrown from inside netcdf library complaining about mismatch for `chunksizes` parameters.

related to #452 (without setting chunksizes compressions settings will be sub-optimal)

I'm working on a library that supports py3.4+, to which I'm adding async (mainly trio) support. Because I can't use `await` and `async` keywords, I use the `coroutine` decorator and `yield from`. This worked fine earlier, however, now using trio master I'm getting an exception.

Is this intentional or a bug? Example code:

from asyncio import coroutine
import trio

def do_something():

trio.run(do_something)

issue_title: pylint does not find open_nursery and run methods
issue_body:
This might not be a trio problem, I am aware of this:

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import trio

async def toddler():
    print('Toddler is playing...')
    await trio.sleep(5)

async def parent():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery: #pylint: no-member
        print('Bringing toddler to nursery...')
        nursery.start_soon(toddler)
    print('Going home...')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    trio.run(parent)

```

In this code above pylint on python 3.7 complains about not finding members:

```
##########Linting Output - pylint##########
************* Module issue
10,15,error,E1101:Module 'trio' has no 'open_nursery' member
17,4,error,E1101:Module 'trio' has no 'run' member

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 1.67/10 (previous run: 1.67/10, +0.00)
```
The issue can obviously worked around by ignoring.

ipython finds both functions with code completion.

Also reported against pylint.
And/or have a sane cache invalidation strategy in place.
See https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-java/pull/550 for encoding format.

## Release version
 0.16.0 (expected to appear in 0.20.0 as well)

## Issue
`ValueError` is raised on [loop/merge.py#L7](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/master/alphatwirl/loop/merge.py#L7) when duplicate datasets (two or more dataset entries with the same `name` attribute)

## Description
Duplicate datasets can appear by user error. The final duplicate dataset will overwrite all the other in [loop/EventDatasetReader.py#L74](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/master/alphatwirl/loop/EventDatasetReader.py#L74), but [loop/EventDatasetReader.py#L68](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/master/alphatwirl/loop/EventDatasetReader.py#L68) is filled with `runids` from all dataset duplicates.

When the code reaches [loop/merge.py#L7](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/master/alphatwirl/loop/merge.py#L7) it tries to access runids from duplicate datasets that have been overwritten in [loop/EventDatasetReader.py#L74](https://github.com/alphatwirl/alphatwirl/blob/master/alphatwirl/loop/EventDatasetReader.py#L74)

## Resolution
Everything works fine if there are no duplicate datasets.

However, this could be checked or enforced somewhere earlier.

issue_title: Squeeze operator attribute "axes" should be optional.
issue_body:
Currently squeeze operator requires attribute "axis"  attribute .(https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#Squeeze)

I think this attribute should be marked optional. As per convention on most frameworks( i.e TF, Pytorch, MXNet), absence of this attribute meant all the dimensions with value "1" will be removed.

This will be useful  fro converters while exporting models to ONNX when "axis" is not specified, else the input shape has to be determined.

@bddppq @houseroad @dzhulgakov

issue_title: Patch for generating xml:base attribute in RDF/XML output
issue_body:
gklyn...@gmail.com, 2011-09-23T14:54:59.000Z
<pre>
I have a need to be able to generate XML/RDF output with a specified xml:base value.

Attached is a patch that implements this for the XMLSerializer and PrettyXMLSerializer modules, with an extra test case forPrettyXMLSerializer, and a new test module for XMLSerializer.

test_serializexml.py is pretty much a copy of the test_prettyxml.py, with the tests tweaked to match the output generated.

Apart from the test cases, the patch contains no documentation for how to use this - I wasn't sure where that should go.  (xml:base is generated by adding an xml_base=... keyword argument to the serialize call.  There is no attempt to align the value with the base provided: my application uses different values for these, as I use a relative base URI.)

All tests pass on my system, which is MacOS 10.5 running Python 2.7:
